examples.explanation: Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5), x ≡ 2 (mod 7). M = 3×5×7 = 105. Step 2: Start x = 0. Check: 0%3=0≠2 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%3=2✓, 2%5=2≠3 → fail. Step 5: Continue checking... x=8: 8%3=2✓, 8%5=3✓, 8%7=1≠2 → fail. Step 6: x=23: 23%3=2✓, 23%5=3✓, 23%7=2✓ → all satisfied. Step 7: Return 23.

Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 1 (mod 2), x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5). M = 2×3×5 = 30. Step 2: Start x = 0. Check: 0%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%2=1✓, 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 5: Continue... x=11: 11%2=1✓, 11%3=2✓, 11%5=1≠3 → fail. Step 6: x=23: 23%2=1✓, 23%3=2✓, 23%5=3✓ → all satisfied. Step 7: Return 23.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N * max(m_i))
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers starting from 0 up to the product of moduli, checking each against all congruences until a solution is found. Use this only for very small inputs due to its inefficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force CRT with Large Constraints
ladders.desc: Find the smallest non-negative integer x satisfying all congruences x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) by checking every integer from 0 up to the product of moduli.
ladders.explanation: The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) problem asks us to find an integer x that satisfies multiple congruence equations simultaneously. The brute force approach is conceptually simple but computationally expensive: we systematically check every possible integer starting from 0 until we find one that satisfies all congruences. The intuition is that if a solution exists, it must be less than the product of all moduli (M = m₁ × m₂ × ... × mₙ) due to the periodic nature of modular arithmetic. We start at 0 and increment by 1, checking each candidate x against all congruences. For each congruence x ≡ a_i (mod m_i), we verify if (x - a_i) is divisible by m_i (i.e., x % m_i == a_i). If x satisfies all congruences, we return it. If we reach M without finding a solution, no solution exists. The time complexity is O(N × M) where N is the number of congruences and M is the product of moduli, making it impractical for large inputs. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This approach connects to the broader problem by providing a baseline solution that's easy to understand but inefficient, highlighting the need for optimized algorithms. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the case where no solution exists (must check up to M), 2) Forgetting that x must be non-negative, 3) Incorrectly implementing the modulo check (ensure it's x % m_i == a_i, not a_i % m_i). Real code pattern: def brute_force_crt(congruences): # congruences = [(a1, m1), (a2, m2), ...]; M = product of all m_i; for x in range(M): if all(x % m == a for a, m in congruences): return x; return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5), x ≡ 2 (mod 7). M = 3×5×7 = 105. Step 2: Start x = 0. Check: 0%3=0≠2 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%3=2✓, 2%5=2≠3 → fail. Step 5: Continue checking... x=8: 8%3=2✓, 8%5=3✓, 8%7=1≠2 → fail. Step 6: x=23: 23%3=2✓, 23%5=3✓, 23%7=2✓ → all satisfied. Step 7: Return 23.

Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 1 (mod 2), x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5). M = 2×3×5 = 30. Step 2: Start x = 0. Check: 0%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%2=1✓, 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 5: Continue... x=11: 11%2=1✓, 11%3=2✓, 11%5=1≠3 → fail. Step 6: x=23: 23%2=1✓, 23%3=2✓, 23%5=3✓ → all satisfied. Step 7: Return 23.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking All Congruences for a Candidate
ladders.desc: Given a candidate integer x and a list of congruences, verify if x satisfies all x ≡ a_i (mod m_i).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step in the brute force CRT algorithm. For a given candidate x, we need to check if it satisfies every congruence equation x ≡ a_i (mod m_i). The intuition is that modular arithmetic defines equivalence classes: x satisfies a congruence if its remainder when divided by m_i equals a_i. We iterate through each congruence (a_i, m_i) and compute x % m_i (the remainder of x divided by m_i). If x % m_i == a_i for all i, then x is a valid solution; otherwise, it fails. The algorithm: 1) Input: integer x, list congruences = [(a1, m1), (a2, m2), ...]. 2) For each (a, m) in congruences: if x % m != a, return False. 3) If all checks pass, return True. Time complexity is O(N) where N is the number of congruences, as we check each once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the inner loop that determines whether a candidate x is valid in the full brute force search. Common mistakes: 1) Using a_i % m_i instead of x % m_i (this checks the wrong value), 2) Not handling cases where a_i >= m_i (though in standard CRT, 0 ≤ a_i < m_i), 3) Early exit on first failure is correct, but ensure all are checked if passing. Real code pattern: def satisfies_all(x, congruences): for a, m in congruences: if x % m != a: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check first congruence: 8 % 3 = 2, matches a=2 → pass. Step 2: Check second: 8 % 5 = 3, matches a=3 → pass. Step 3: Check third: 8 % 7 = 1, does not match a=2 → fail. Step 4: Return False.
Step 1: Check first: 23 % 3 = 2, matches 2 → pass. Step 2: Check second: 23 % 5 = 3, matches 3 → pass. Step 3: Check third: 23 % 7 = 2, matches 2 → pass. Step 4: All passed, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Search Up to Product Bound
ladders.desc: Iterate through integers from 0 to M-1 (where M is product of moduli) to find the smallest x satisfying all congruences.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the search strategy that forms the backbone of the brute force approach. The intuition is based on the property of the Chinese Remainder Theorem: if a solution exists, it is unique modulo M (the product of all moduli). Therefore, we only need to check integers in the range [0, M-1]. By starting at 0 and incrementing by 1, we guarantee finding the smallest non-negative solution. The algorithm: 1) Input: list congruences = [(a1, m1), (a2, m2), ...]. 2) Compute M = m₁ × m₂ × ... × mₙ. 3) For x from 0 to M-1: if x satisfies all congruences (using the L1 sub-routine), return x. 4) If no x found, return None (indicating no solution). Time complexity is O(M × N) in worst case, as we may check all M values and for each, check N congruences. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as the outer loop that drives the full solution, and to L1 by calling the verification function for each x. Common mistakes: 1) Going up to M instead of M-1 (since solution is modulo M, x = M is equivalent to x = 0), 2) Not computing M correctly (ensure integer multiplication), 3) Forgetting to handle the no-solution case (though CRT guarantees solution if moduli are pairwise coprime, this brute force works even if not, but may not find one). Real code pattern: def iterative_search(congruences): M = 1; for _, m in congruences: M *= m; for x in range(M): if satisfies_all(x, congruences): return x; return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5). M = 3×5 = 15. Step 2: Start x=0: 0%3=0≠2 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%3=2✓, 2%5=2≠3 → fail. Step 5: Continue... x=8: 8%3=2✓, 8%5=3✓ → all satisfied. Step 6: Return 8.

Step 1: Congruences: x ≡ 1 (mod 2), x ≡ 2 (mod 3). M = 2×3 = 6. Step 2: Start x=0: 0%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 3: x=1: 1%2=1✓, 1%3=1≠2 → fail. Step 4: x=2: 2%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 5: x=3: 3%2=1✓, 3%3=0≠2 → fail. Step 6: x=4: 4%2=0≠1 → fail. Step 7: x=5: 5%2=1✓, 5%3=2✓ → all satisfied. Step 8: Return 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Product of Moduli
ladders.desc: Calculate M = m₁ × m₂ × ... × mₙ, the product of all modulus values in the congruences.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for determining the search bound in the brute force CRT algorithm. The product M serves as an upper limit for the solution search because, by the Chinese Remainder Theorem, any solution x is unique modulo M. Intuitively, modular arithmetic creates cycles: for a modulus m, the remainders repeat every m numbers. When combining multiple moduli, the combined cycle length is the least common multiple (LCM) of the moduli. If the moduli are pairwise coprime, LCM equals their product M. Even if not coprime, M is a multiple of the LCM, so checking up to M-1 is safe (though may be inefficient). The algorithm is straightforward: initialize M = 1, then iterate through each modulus m_i in the congruences and multiply M by m_i. Time complexity is O(N) where N is the number of congruences. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the M value used in the iterative search loop. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to initialize M as 1 (starting at 0 would give product 0), 2) Integer overflow for large moduli (in real applications, use big integers or modular arithmetic, but here we assume standard integers), 3) Misunderstanding that M is the product, not the sum or another combination. Real code pattern: def product_of_moduli(congruences): M = 1; for _, m in congruences: M *= m; return M.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with M = 1. Step 2: First modulus: m1 = 3, M = 1 × 3 = 3. Step 3: Second modulus: m2 = 5, M = 3 × 5 = 15. Step 4: Third modulus: m3 = 7, M = 15 × 7 = 105. Step 5: Return 105.
Step 1: Start M = 1. Step 2: m1 = 2, M = 1 × 2 = 2. Step 3: m2 = 3, M = 2 × 3 = 6. Step 4: m3 = 4, M = 6 × 4 = 24. Step 5: Return 24.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation Check
ladders.desc: Given integers x, a, and m, check if x ≡ a (mod m) by verifying x % m == a.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the atomic unit of verifying a single congruence in the CRT problem. The modulo operation x % m computes the remainder when x is divided by m. In modular arithmetic, x ≡ a (mod m) means that x and a have the same remainder when divided by m, i.e., x % m equals a % m. Since in standard CRT inputs, a is already in the range [0, m-1], we can simply check if x % m == a. Intuition: Think of a clock with m hours: x % m tells you where the hour hand points after x hours, and it should point to a if the congruence holds. The algorithm: 1) Input: integers x, a, m. 2) Compute remainder = x % m. 3) Return True if remainder == a, else False. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L1 as the basic check performed for each congruence in the verification sub-routine. Common mistakes: 1) Using a % m instead of x % m (this would check if a is congruent to itself, which is always true), 2) Not handling negative x (in this context, x is non-negative, but in general, programming languages define % differently for negatives; we assume non-negative x), 3) Confusing modulo with division (e.g., using x / m). Real code pattern: def check_congruence(x, a, m): return x % m == a.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute x % m: 8 ÷ 3 = 2 remainder 2, so 8 % 3 = 2. Step 2: Compare remainder with a: 2 == 2. Step 3: Return True.
Step 1: Compute x % m: 8 ÷ 7 = 1 remainder 1, so 8 % 7 = 1. Step 2: Compare remainder with a: 1 != 2. Step 3: Return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Modular Arithmetic and Congruence
ladders.desc: Learn the basic idea of modulo operation and what x ≡ a (mod m) means.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the entire CRT problem. Modular arithmetic is like clock arithmetic: after reaching m, numbers wrap around. The expression x ≡ a (mod m) is read as 'x is congruent to a modulo m' and means that when x is divided by m, the remainder is a (or more formally, m divides x - a). Intuition: Imagine a circle with m points labeled 0 to m-1. Starting at 0, moving x steps around the circle lands you at the same point as moving a steps, because the circle repeats every m steps. For example, on a 12-hour clock, 15 ≡ 3 (mod 12) because both 15 and 3 land at 3 o'clock. Key properties: 1) a is always in the range 0 ≤ a < m (the standard representation). 2) x can be any integer, but x % m gives its representative in [0, m-1]. The modulo operation x % m computes the remainder: e.g., 17 % 5 = 2 because 17 = 3×5 + 2. This connects to L4 as the core operation used to check congruences. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking x ≡ a (mod m) means x = a (it means they have the same remainder, not that they are equal), 2) Confusing modulo with division remainder (in programming, % gives remainder, which matches modular arithmetic for non-negative numbers), 3) Not understanding that congruence is an equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, transitive). Real code pattern: In Python, use x % m to get the remainder; no function needed for basic understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 7 ÷ 5: 5 goes into 7 once with remainder 2. Step 2: So 7 % 5 = 2. Step 3: Since the remainder 2 equals the given a=2, the congruence holds. Step 4: This means 7 and 2 have the same remainder when divided by 5.

Step 1: Compute 10 ÷ 3: 3 goes into 10 three times (3×3=9) with remainder 1. Step 2: So 10 % 3 = 1. Step 3: Since remainder 1 equals a=1, the congruence holds. Step 4: On a clock with 3 hours, 10 steps lands at the same point as 1 step.

approaches.name: Extended Euclidean Algorithm O(N log M)
approaches.summary: Solve the system pairwise by combining congruences using the extended Euclidean algorithm to find modular inverses and update the solution incrementally. This is efficient for moderately large inputs with pairwise coprime moduli.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Solve CRT System with Large Constraints
ladders.desc: Given N congruences x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) with pairwise coprime moduli, find the smallest non-negative solution using pairwise combination via extended Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) states that for pairwise coprime moduli m_i, there exists a unique solution modulo M = ∏m_i. For large constraints, we need an efficient O(N log M) algorithm. The intuition is to combine congruences pairwise: given two congruences x ≡ a1 (mod m1) and x ≡ a2 (mod m2), we find k such that a1 + k*m1 ≡ a2 (mod m2). This requires solving m1*k ≡ (a2 - a1) (mod m2) for k, which is a linear congruence. Using the extended Euclidean algorithm, we find the modular inverse of m1 modulo m2 (since gcd(m1,m2)=1), then compute k = ((a2 - a1) * inv) mod m2. The combined congruence becomes x ≡ a1 + k*m1 (mod m1*m2). We repeat this process for all congruences, updating the solution incrementally. The algorithm: 1. Initialize solution = a1, modulus = m1. 2. For i from 2 to N: compute diff = (a_i - solution) mod m_i, compute inv = modular_inverse(modulus, m_i) using extended Euclidean algorithm, compute k = (diff * inv) mod m_i, update solution = solution + k * modulus, update modulus = modulus * m_i, reduce solution modulo modulus. 3. Return solution. Time complexity is O(N log M) because each extended Euclidean call takes O(log min(modulus, m_i)). Space is O(1). This feeds directly from L1's modular inverse function. Common mistakes: not handling negative differences properly (use modulo), forgetting that moduli must be pairwise coprime, and integer overflow with large products (use 64-bit integers).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize solution = 2, modulus = 3. Step 2: Combine with x ≡ 3 (mod 5). diff = (3 - 2) mod 5 = 1. inv = modular_inverse(3,5) = 2 (since 3*2=6≡1 mod5). k = (1*2) mod5 = 2. solution = 2 + 2*3 = 8. modulus = 3*5=15. solution mod15 = 8. Step 3: Combine with x ≡ 2 (mod 7). diff = (2 - 8) mod7 = (-6) mod7 = 1. inv = modular_inverse(15,7). gcd(15,7): 15=7*2+1, so 1=15-7*2, inverse of 15 mod7 is -2 mod7 = 5. k = (1*5) mod7 = 5. solution = 8 + 5*15 = 83. modulus = 15*7=105. solution mod105 = 83. But 83 satisfies all? 83 mod3=2, mod5=3, mod7=6? Wait error: 83 mod7=6, not 2. Let's recompute: diff = (2 - 8) mod7 = -6 mod7 = 1 (correct). inv: extended Euclidean for 15,7: 15=7*2+1, so 1=15*1 + 7*(-2), inverse of 15 mod7 is 1 mod7 = 1? Actually from 1=15*1 + 7*(-2), modulo 7: 1 ≡ 15*1 (mod7), so inverse is 1. Let's compute properly: We need inv such that 15*inv ≡ 1 (mod7). 15 mod7=1, so inv=1. k = 1*1 mod7=1. solution = 8 + 1*15 = 23. modulus=105. 23 mod3=2, mod5=3, mod7=2. Correct.

Step 1: solution=1, modulus=2. Step 2: Combine x≡2 mod3. diff=(2-1) mod3=1. inv=modular_inverse(2,3)=2 (since 2*2=4≡1 mod3). k=1*2 mod3=2. solution=1+2*2=5. modulus=2*3=6. solution mod6=5. Step 3: Combine x≡3 mod5. diff=(3-5) mod5=-2 mod5=3. inv=modular_inverse(6,5). gcd(6,5): 6=5*1+1, so 1=6-5*1, inverse of 6 mod5 is 1 mod5=1. k=3*1 mod5=3. solution=5+3*6=23. modulus=6*5=30. solution mod30=23. Verify: 23 mod2=1, mod3=2, mod5=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modular Inverse via Extended Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Given two coprime integers a and m, find x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m).
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse is crucial for solving linear congruences in CRT. The intuition: since a and m are coprime (gcd=1), Bézout's identity guarantees integers x,y such that a*x + m*y = 1. Taking modulo m, a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), so x is the inverse. The extended Euclidean algorithm computes gcd(a,m) and coefficients x,y. Algorithm: 1. Initialize old_r=a, r=m, old_s=1, s=0, old_t=0, t=1. 2. While r≠0: quotient = old_r // r, (old_r, r) = (r, old_r - quotient*r), (old_s, s) = (s, old_s - quotient*s), (old_t, t) = (t, old_t - quotient*t). 3. gcd=old_r, x=old_s, y=old_t. 4. The inverse is x mod m (ensure positive). Time O(log min(a,m)), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the inverse needed to combine congruences. Common mistakes: not checking coprimality (inverse exists only if gcd=1), not taking modulo m at the end (x might be negative), and integer overflow in intermediate steps (use 64-bit).

examples.trace: Extended Euclidean: a=3, m=11. Initialize: old_r=3, r=11, old_s=1, s=0, old_t=0, t=1. Iteration1: quotient=3//11=0, update: old_r=11, r=3-0*11=3, old_s=0, s=1-0*0=1, old_t=1, t=0-0*1=0. Now old_r=11, r=3. Iteration2: quotient=11//3=3, update: old_r=3, r=11-3*3=2, old_s=1, s=0-3*1=-3, old_t=0, t=1-3*0=1. Now old_r=3, r=2. Iteration3: quotient=3//2=1, update: old_r=2, r=3-1*2=1, old_s=-3, s=1-1*(-3)=4, old_t=1, t=0-1*1=-1. Now old_r=2, r=1. Iteration4: quotient=2//1=2, update: old_r=1, r=2-2*1=0, old_s=4, s=-3-2*4=-11, old_t=-1, t=1-2*(-1)=3. r=0 stop. gcd=old_r=1, x=old_s=4, y=old_t=-1. Inverse is 4 mod11=4. Verify: 3*4=12≡1 mod11.

a=5, m=12. Initialize: old_r=5, r=12, old_s=1, s=0, old_t=0, t=1. Iter1: quotient=5//12=0, update: old_r=12, r=5, old_s=0, s=1, old_t=1, t=0. Iter2: quotient=12//5=2, update: old_r=5, r=12-2*5=2, old_s=1, s=0-2*1=-2, old_t=0, t=1-2*0=1. Iter3: quotient=5//2=2, update: old_r=2, r=5-2*2=1, old_s=-2, s=1-2*(-2)=5, old_t=1, t=0-2*1=-2. Iter4: quotient=2//1=2, update: old_r=1, r=2-2*1=0, old_s=5, s=-2-2*5=-12, old_t=-2, t=1-2*(-2)=5. Stop. gcd=1, x=5, inverse=5 mod12=5. Verify: 5*5=25≡1 mod12.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Combination of Two Congruences
ladders.desc: Given two congruences x ≡ a1 (mod m1) and x ≡ a2 (mod m2) with gcd(m1,m2)=1, find the combined congruence x ≡ A (mod M) where M=m1*m2.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of CRT is that we can merge two congruences into one. Intuition: We need x such that x = a1 + k*m1 for some integer k, and also x ≡ a2 (mod m2). Substituting: a1 + k*m1 ≡ a2 (mod m2) => m1*k ≡ (a2 - a1) (mod m2). This is a linear congruence in k. Since gcd(m1,m2)=1, m1 has an inverse modulo m2, so k ≡ (a2 - a1) * inv(m1) (mod m2). Compute k, then x = a1 + k*m1 satisfies both congruences. The new modulus is M=m1*m2 because the solution repeats every LCM(m1,m2)=m1*m2 (since coprime). Algorithm: 1. Compute diff = (a2 - a1) mod m2. 2. Compute inv = modular_inverse(m1, m2). 3. Compute k = (diff * inv) mod m2. 4. Compute x = a1 + k*m1. 5. The combined congruence is x mod (m1*m2). Time O(log min(m1,m2)) for inverse, space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the iterative step. Common mistakes: using wrong modulus for diff (should be m2), not reducing x modulo M, and assuming k can be any integer (it's modulo m2).

examples.trace: a1=2, m1=3, a2=3, m2=5. Step1: diff = (3-2) mod5 = 1. Step2: inv = modular_inverse(3,5)=2 (since 3*2=6≡1 mod5). Step3: k = (1*2) mod5 = 2. Step4: x = 2 + 2*3 = 8. Step5: modulus M=3*5=15. Combined: x ≡ 8 (mod15). Verify: 8 mod3=2, mod5=3.

a1=1, m1=4, a2=2, m2=7. Step1: diff = (2-1) mod7 = 1. Step2: inv = modular_inverse(4,7). 4*2=8≡1 mod7, so inv=2. Step3: k = (1*2) mod7 = 2. Step4: x = 1 + 2*4 = 9. Step5: M=4*7=28. Combined: x ≡ 9 (mod28). Verify: 9 mod4=1, mod7=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Solving Linear Congruence a*x ≡ b (mod m)
ladders.desc: Given integers a,b,m with gcd(a,m)=1, find x such that a*x ≡ b (mod m).
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental step in CRT combination. Intuition: Since a and m are coprime, a has an inverse modulo m. Multiplying both sides by this inverse gives x ≡ b * inv(a) (mod m). The solution is unique modulo m. Algorithm: 1. Compute inv = modular_inverse(a, m) using extended Euclidean algorithm. 2. Compute x = (b * inv) mod m. 3. Return x. Time O(log min(a,m)) for inverse, space O(1). This feeds into L2 by solving m1*k ≡ diff (mod m2) for k. Common mistakes: not checking gcd(a,m)=1 (no solution if not coprime), not reducing b modulo m first, and integer overflow in multiplication (use modulo operation during computation).

examples.trace: a=3, b=4, m=7. gcd(3,7)=1. Step1: inv = modular_inverse(3,7). 3*5=15≡1 mod7, so inv=5. Step2: x = (4*5) mod7 = 20 mod7 = 6. Verify: 3*6=18≡4 mod7.
a=5, b=2, m=9. gcd(5,9)=1. Step1: inv = modular_inverse(5,9). 5*2=10≡1 mod9, so inv=2. Step2: x = (2*2) mod9 = 4 mod9 = 4. Verify: 5*4=20≡2 mod9.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Extended Euclidean Algorithm for GCD and Coefficients
ladders.desc: Given integers a and b, compute gcd(a,b) and integers x,y such that a*x + b*y = gcd(a,b).
ladders.explanation: This algorithm extends the Euclidean algorithm to find Bézout coefficients. Intuition: The Euclidean algorithm computes gcd by repeated subtraction (or division). By keeping track of how each remainder is expressed as a linear combination of a and b, we can work backwards to find x,y. Algorithm: 1. Initialize old_r=a, r=b, old_s=1, s=0, old_t=0, t=1. 2. While r≠0: quotient = old_r // r, (old_r, r) = (r, old_r - quotient*r), (old_s, s) = (s, old_s - quotient*s), (old_t, t) = (t, old_t - quotient*t). 3. gcd=old_r, x=old_s, y=old_t. Time O(log min(a,b)), space O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the inverse when gcd=1 (if a*x + m*y=1, then a*x ≡1 mod m). Common mistakes: swapping a and b (algorithm works either way), not initializing coefficients correctly, and integer overflow in quotient multiplication (use 64-bit).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Understand congruence modulo n: a ≡ b (mod n) means n divides (a-b).
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic is the foundation for CRT. Intuition: Numbers wrap around after reaching n, like a clock. a ≡ b (mod n) if a and b have the same remainder when divided by n. Key properties: 1. Addition: (a+b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n. 2. Subtraction: (a-b) mod n = ((a mod n) - (b mod n)) mod n (ensure non-negative). 3. Multiplication: (a*b) mod n = ((a mod n) * (b mod n)) mod n. 4. Division is tricky: a/b mod n requires modular inverse if gcd(b,n)=1. Algorithm for basic operations: always reduce modulo n to avoid large numbers. Time O(1) per operation, space O(1). This feeds into L4 by defining the modulo operation used in Euclidean algorithm. Common mistakes: forgetting to take modulo after each operation, mishandling negative numbers (use (x%n + n)%n), and confusing congruence with equality.

examples.trace: Step1: 17 mod5 = 2, 23 mod5 = 3. Step2: (2+3) mod5 = 5 mod5 = 0. Alternatively, 17+23=40, 40 mod5=0.

Step1: 12 mod8 = 4, 27 mod8 = 3. Step2: (4-3) mod8 = 1 mod8 = 1? Wait, careful: 12-27=-15. To compute mod: -15 mod8 = (-15 + 8*2) mod8 = (-15+16) mod8 = 1 mod8 = 1. But using reduction: (12 mod8 - 27 mod8) mod8 = (4-3) mod8 = 1 mod8 = 1. That matches. However, let's verify: -15 divided by 8 gives quotient -2, remainder 1? Actually, remainder should be non-negative: -15 = 8*(-2) + 1, so remainder 1. So output is 1. But in example, I said 5, which is wrong. Correcting: output should be 1. Trace: 12-27=-15. -15 mod8: add 8 until non-negative: -15+16=1, so 1.

approaches.name: Garner's Algorithm O(N^2 log M)
approaches.summary: Use Garner's algorithm to compute the solution in mixed radix form without requiring the moduli to be pairwise coprime, by solving for coefficients using modular inverses. This is optimal for non-coprime moduli or when intermediate results must be kept small.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Garner's Algorithm for CRT with Large Constraints
ladders.desc: Implement Garner's algorithm to solve the Chinese Remainder Theorem problem for large inputs with non-coprime moduli.
ladders.explanation: Garner's algorithm solves the system of congruences x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) by expressing x in mixed radix form: x = v_0 + v_1*m_0 + v_2*m_0*m_1 + ... + v_{n-1}*m_0*m_1*...*m_{n-2}. The key insight is that we can compute coefficients v_i recursively while keeping intermediate results small by working modulo the product of previous moduli. For each i from 0 to n-1, we compute v_i = (a_i - Σ_{j=0}^{i-1} v_j * M_j) * inv(M_i) mod m_i, where M_j = Π_{k=0}^{j-1} m_k. This requires modular inverses of M_i modulo m_i, which exist only if gcd(M_i, m_i) = 1. The algorithm handles non-coprime moduli by checking this condition and returning -1 if unsatisfied. Time complexity is O(N² log M) due to computing products and inverses, and space is O(N) for storing coefficients and products. This approach is optimal for large constraints as it avoids large intermediate numbers by working modulo individual m_i. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the modular inverse computation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to check gcd conditions for inverses, integer overflow in product calculations, and incorrect indexing in the summation. Real code pattern: def garner(a, m): n = len(a); v = [0]*n; M = [1]*n; for i in range(n): temp = a[i]; for j in range(i): temp -= v[j]*M[j]; temp %= m[i]; inv = mod_inverse(M[i]%m[i], m[i]); if inv is None: return -1; v[i] = temp*inv % m[i]; if i < n-1: M[i+1] = M[i]*m[i]; result = 0; for i in range(n): result += v[i]*M[i]; return result;

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize v=[0,0,0], M=[1,1,1]. i=0: temp=a[0]=1; no previous j; temp=1 mod 2=1; inv=mod_inverse(1,2)=1; v[0]=1*1 mod 2=1; M[1]=1*2=2. i=1: temp=a[1]=2; j=0: temp-=v[0]*M[0]=2-1*1=1; temp=1 mod 3=1; inv=mod_inverse(M[1]%3=2,3)=2 (since 2*2=4≡1 mod3); v[1]=1*2 mod 3=2; M[2]=2*3=6. i=2: temp=a[2]=3; j=0: temp-=v[0]*M[0]=3-1*1=2; j=1: temp-=v[1]*M[1]=2-2*2=-2; temp=-2 mod 6=4; inv=mod_inverse(M[2]%6=6%6=0,6): gcd(0,6)=6≠1, so no inverse exists. Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modular Inverse Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the modular inverse of a number modulo m using the extended Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse of a modulo m is an integer x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m). It exists if and only if gcd(a, m) = 1. The extended Euclidean algorithm computes gcd(a, m) and coefficients x, y such that a*x + m*y = gcd(a, m). If gcd(a, m) = 1, then a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), so x mod m is the inverse. Intuition: The algorithm recursively reduces the problem by expressing gcd(a, m) in terms of gcd(m, a mod m), building coefficients through back-substitution. Step-by-step: function mod_inverse(a, m): if m == 1: return 0; g, x, y = egcd(a, m); if g != 1: return None; else: return x % m. Where egcd(a, b): if a == 0: return (b, 0, 1); else: g, x1, y1 = egcd(b % a, a); x = y1 - (b // a) * x1; y = x1; return (g, x, y). Time complexity is O(log min(a, m)) due to the Euclidean algorithm, and space is O(log min(a, m)) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine is used in Garner's algorithm to compute inverses needed for coefficient calculations. Common mistakes include not handling m=1, forgetting to check gcd=1, and not taking x modulo m to ensure positive result. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, m): def egcd(a, b): if a == 0: return b, 0, 1; g, x1, y1 = egcd(b % a, a); return g, y1 - (b // a) * x1, x1; g, x, _ = egcd(a, m); if g != 1: return None; return x % m.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call egcd(3,7). Since 3≠0, compute egcd(7%3=1,3). Step 2: egcd(1,3): since 1≠0, compute egcd(3%1=0,1). Step 3: egcd(0,1): base case, return (1,0,1). Step 4: Back to egcd(1,3): g=1, x1=0, y1=1; compute x = y1 - (3//1)*x1 = 1 - 3*0 = 1; y = x1 = 0; return (1,1,0). Step 5: Back to egcd(3,7): g=1, x1=1, y1=0; compute x = y1 - (7//3)*x1 = 0 - 2*1 = -2; y = x1 = 1; return (1,-2,1). Step 6: mod_inverse gets g=1, x=-2; since g=1, return x % 7 = -2 % 7 = 5. Check: 3*5=15≡1 mod7.

Step 1: Call egcd(4,6). Since 4≠0, compute egcd(6%4=2,4). Step 2: egcd(2,4): since 2≠0, compute egcd(4%2=0,2). Step 3: egcd(0,2): base case, return (2,0,1). Step 4: Back to egcd(2,4): g=2, x1=0, y1=1; compute x = y1 - (4//2)*x1 = 1 - 2*0 = 1; y = x1 = 0; return (2,1,0). Step 5: Back to egcd(4,6): g=2, x1=1, y1=0; compute x = y1 - (6//4)*x1 = 0 - 1*1 = -1; y = x1 = 1; return (2,-1,1). Step 6: mod_inverse gets g=2; since g≠1, return None. Check: gcd(4,6)=2≠1, so no inverse exists.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mixed Radix Representation for CRT
ladders.desc: Express the solution to a system of congruences in mixed radix form to compute coefficients recursively.
ladders.explanation: The mixed radix representation expresses a number x as x = v_0 + v_1*m_0 + v_2*m_0*m_1 + ... + v_{n-1}*m_0*m_1*...*m_{n-2}, where m_i are the moduli and v_i are coefficients to be determined. For a system x ≡ a_i (mod m_i), we can solve for v_i sequentially. Intuition: By working modulo m_i, we isolate v_i while accounting for contributions from previous terms. Specifically, for each i, we have x ≡ a_i (mod m_i). Substituting the mixed radix form: v_0 + v_1*m_0 + ... + v_{i-1}*M_{i-1} + v_i*M_i ≡ a_i (mod m_i), where M_j = Π_{k=0}^{j-1} m_k. Since all terms except v_i*M_i are known from previous steps, we rearrange to v_i ≡ (a_i - Σ_{j=0}^{i-1} v_j*M_j) * inv(M_i) (mod m_i). This allows computing v_i without large numbers by working modulo m_i. Step-by-step: Initialize v array and M array with M[0]=1. For i from 0 to n-1: compute temp = a_i - Σ_{j=0}^{i-1} v_j*M_j mod m_i; compute inv = modular inverse of M_i mod m_i; v_i = temp * inv mod m_i; update M[i+1] = M[i]*m_i. Time complexity O(N²) for the summations, and space O(N). Connection to L0: This logic is the heart of Garner's algorithm, using modular inverses from L1. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing in M array, not reducing modulo m_i at each step, and misunderstanding the mixed radix form. Real code pattern: v = [0]*n; M = [1]*n; for i in range(n): temp = a[i]; for j in range(i): temp -= v[j]*M[j]; temp %= m[i]; v[i] = temp * mod_inverse(M[i]%m[i], m[i]) % m[i]; if i < n-1: M[i+1] = M[i]*m[i];

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize v=[0,0], M=[1,1]. i=0: temp=a[0]=2; no previous j; temp=2 mod 3=2; inv=mod_inverse(M[0]%3=1,3)=1; v[0]=2*1 mod 3=2; M[1]=M[0]*m[0]=1*3=3. i=1: temp=a[1]=3; j=0: temp-=v[0]*M[0]=3-2*1=1; temp=1 mod 5=1; inv=mod_inverse(M[1]%5=3,5)=2; v[1]=1*2 mod 5=2. Final v=[2,2], M=[1,3].

Step 1: Initialize v=[0,0,0], M=[1,1,1]. i=0: temp=a[0]=1; no j; temp=1 mod 2=1; inv=mod_inverse(1,2)=1; v[0]=1*1 mod 2=1; M[1]=1*2=2. i=1: temp=a[1]=0; j=0: temp-=v[0]*M[0]=0-1*1=-1; temp=-1 mod 3=2; inv=mod_inverse(M[1]%3=2,3)=2; v[1]=2*2 mod 3=1; M[2]=2*3=6. i=2: temp=a[2]=1; j=0: temp-=v[0]*M[0]=1-1*1=0; j=1: temp-=v[1]*M[1]=0-1*2=-2; temp=-2 mod 5=3; inv=mod_inverse(M[2]%5=6%5=1,5)=1; v[2]=3*1 mod 5=3. Correction: v[1] should be 1? Recompute i=1: temp=0-1*1=-1 mod3=2; inv=2; v[1]=2*2=4 mod3=1. Yes v[1]=1. i=2: temp=1-1*1=0; 0-1*2=-2 mod5=3; inv=1; v[2]=3. So v=[1,1,3], M=[1,2,6]. Wait, check: For i=1, v[1]=1, but in example output it says v=[1,2,1]. Let's recalc carefully: i=0: v0=1. i=1: temp=0-1*1=-1 mod3=2; inv of 2 mod3 is 2 since 2*2=4≡1; v1=2*2=4 mod3=1. So v1=1, not 2. Perhaps error in example output. I'll correct: Output v=[1,1,3], M=[1,2,6]. But to match, let's use a different: For a=[1,2,3], m=[2,3,5]: i=0: v0=1; i=1: temp=2-1*1=1 mod3=1; inv of 2 mod3=2; v1=1*2=2 mod3=2; i=2: temp=3-1*1=2; 2-2*2=2-4=-2 mod5=3; inv of 6 mod5=1 mod5=1; v2=3*1=3. So v=[1,2,3], M=[1,2,6]. I'll use this: Input a=[1,2,3], m=[2,3,5], output v=[1,2,3], M=[1,2,6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic with Large Numbers
ladders.desc: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, and modulo operations on large integers while avoiding overflow.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic involves operations on integers modulo m, ensuring results stay within [0, m-1]. For large numbers, direct computation can cause overflow, so we use properties: (a + b) mod m = ((a mod m) + (b mod m)) mod m; (a - b) mod m = ((a mod m) - (b mod m)) mod m (adjust negative results by adding m); (a * b) mod m = ((a mod m) * (b mod m)) mod m. Intuition: By reducing operands modulo m first, we keep numbers small. Subtraction may yield negatives, so we add m to get a positive remainder. Step-by-step for subtraction: compute diff = (a % m - b % m) % m; if diff < 0, diff += m. For multiplication, use modular multiplication to prevent intermediate overflow. In languages with big integers, direct operations are safe, but for efficiency, reduce early. Time complexity O(1) per operation, space O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in computing temp values and coefficients in mixed radix representation. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle negative results in subtraction, not reducing modulo m before operations, and integer overflow in multiplication. Real code pattern: def mod_add(a, b, m): return (a % m + b % m) % m; def mod_sub(a, b, m): diff = (a % m - b % m) % m; return diff if diff >= 0 else diff + m; def mod_mul(a, b, m): return (a % m * b % m) % m;

examples.output: 0 for addition, 2 for subtraction, 2 for multiplication
examples.trace: Addition: a mod m = 7 % 3 = 1; b mod m = 5 % 3 = 2; (1 + 2) % 3 = 3 % 3 = 0. Subtraction: a mod m = 1; b mod m = 2; (1 - 2) % 3 = -1 % 3 = -1; since negative, add m: -1 + 3 = 2. Multiplication: a mod m = 1; b mod m = 2; (1 * 2) % 3 = 2 % 3 = 2.

examples.output: 3 for addition, 1 for subtraction, 2 for multiplication
examples.trace: Addition: a mod m = 100 % 7 = 2; b mod m = 50 % 7 = 1; (2 + 1) % 7 = 3 % 7 = 3. Subtraction: a mod m = 2; b mod m = 1; (2 - 1) % 7 = 1 % 7 = 1 (positive). Multiplication: a mod m = 2; b mod m = 1; (2 * 1) % 7 = 2 % 7 = 2. Wait, correction: 100*50=5000; 5000 mod7: 7*714=4998, remainder 2. But via reduction: 2*1=2 mod7=2. So multiplication output 2, not 6. I'll correct: Output for multiplication is 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Intuition: If d divides a and b, then it also divides a - q*b for any integer q, including when q = floor(a/b), so a mod b. By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the numbers until the remainder is 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. Step-by-step: function gcd(a, b): while b ≠ 0: temp = b; b = a % b; a = temp; return a. For example, gcd(48,18): b=18, a% b=48%18=12, set a=18, b=12; next: b=12, 18%12=6, a=12, b=6; next: b=6, 12%6=0, a=6, b=0; return 6. Time complexity O(log min(a, b)) as numbers reduce quickly, space O(1). Connection to L3: GCD is used to check if modular inverse exists (gcd must be 1). Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if b=0 initially, and confusion with the order of operations. Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): a, b = abs(a), abs(b); while b: a, b = b, a % b; return a;

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Since b≠0, compute a % b = 48 % 18 = 12. Set a=18, b=12. Step 2: b=12≠0, compute 18 % 12 = 6. Set a=12, b=6. Step 3: b=6≠0, compute 12 % 6 = 0. Set a=6, b=0. Step 4: b=0, so return a=6.
Step 1: a=17, b=13. b≠0, compute 17 % 13 = 4. Set a=13, b=4. Step 2: b=4≠0, compute 13 % 4 = 1. Set a=4, b=1. Step 3: b=1≠0, compute 4 % 1 = 0. Set a=1, b=0. Step 4: b=0, return a=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Understand the concept of congruence modulo m and basic properties.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic deals with integers where numbers wrap around after reaching a modulus m. We say a is congruent to b modulo m, written a ≡ b (mod m), if m divides (a - b). Intuition: Think of a clock with m hours: times that differ by multiples of m are considered the same. For example, on a 12-hour clock, 15 ≡ 3 (mod 12) because 15 - 3 = 12 is divisible by 12. Key properties: Reflexivity: a ≡ a (mod m); Symmetry: if a ≡ b (mod m), then b ≡ a (mod m); Transitivity: if a ≡ b and b ≡ c, then a ≡ c (mod m). Operations: (a + b) mod m gives the remainder when a+b is divided by m; similarly for subtraction and multiplication. The modulo operation a mod m yields the remainder in [0, m-1]. Step-by-step for computing 17 mod 5: divide 17 by 5 to get quotient 3 and remainder 2, so 17 mod 5 = 2. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation is used in GCD and all higher-level modular operations. Common mistakes include confusing congruence with equality, mishandling negative numbers in modulo (e.g., -3 mod 5 should be 2, not -3), and not reducing results to the proper range. Real code pattern: In Python, a % m gives the remainder in [0, m-1] for positive m. For negative a, (-3) % 5 = 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 17 by 5: 5 * 3 = 15, remainder 17 - 15 = 2. So 17 mod 5 = 2.
Step 1: To find -3 mod 5, add multiples of 5 until result is in [0,4]. -3 + 5 = 2, which is in range. So -3 mod 5 = 2. Alternatively, in Python: (-3) % 5 = 2.
